quotes.topic: Downstep modulation — cycle of fifths vs. chromatic root motion
quotes.verbatim_quote:
A chord progression is generally thought to be a succession of chords, often alternating between

two types of chord structures, with the chord roots progressing, say, through the cycle of fifths or

downward chromatically.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces the concept of downstep modulation by first establishing the conventional root-motion baseline (cycle of fifths, chromatic descent) against which the stationary-root permutation is then contrasted.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
It is frequently the case, however, that the root will remain stationary through two or more chords,
while the chord type on that same root will change. For example:

CM7 Cm? F7 BD M7B?m? Eb7 AbM7 OR: DM7 D7 GM7 G7 CM7

nd

Such a permutation in chord type will usually cause a change in chord function as well, so that the
preceding examples might be given as follows:

IM7 im? V7? IM? m7 V7 IM? AND: IM7 WV? IM7 V7 IM7

C: BP: Ap: D:  G: Cc:

The following patterns will focus on permutations of this sort, where the root remains the same
for rwo or more successive chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the core concept of the chapter's remaining patterns: a chord-type change on a held root that simultaneously shifts harmonic function and key center — the essential mechanic of turnaround/turnback writing.
